figures.caption: Fig. 2. Overview of SEM-RAG. Module 4.1 extracts hierarchical paragraph primitives Ti, conditional-logic table subgraphs Ci, and formula graphs Fi, which are merged into the base multimodal graph G0. Module 4.2 compresses G0 into the hierarchical encoding tree P∗. Module 4.3 aligns topological features zv and textual features tv . At query time, the complexity router selects a retrieval path and produces candidate subgraph Gq for grounded generation.
Fig. 4. Indexing-time scalability comparison. SEM shifts hierarchy construction from LLM calls to graph optimization, which keeps the token budget small as corpus size grows.

Fig. 1. Conceptual comparison between a standard text-centric GraphRAG pipeline and SEM-RAG. In the left panel, standard GraphRAG starts from onedimensional text chunking, loses structural relations in tables and formulas, and then relies on LLM-driven clustering over an unstructured knowledge graph. In the right panel, SEM-RAG first applies a layout-aware parser that converts tables into condition graphs and formulas into operator graphs, then uses Structural Entropy Minimization (SEM) to build a compact hierarchy that supports factoid, relational, and global-summary retrieval.

Fig. 3. Performance on table-heavy and formula-heavy questions, together with manually annotated error categories. SEM-RAG gains are concentrated on cases where preserving header bindings or operator structure matters.
TABLE I POSITIONING OF SEM-RAG AGAINST REPRESENTATIVE RETRIEVAL PARADIGMS FOR TELECOM STANDARDS.

Fig. 5. Breakdown of online latency into retrieval, alignment, and generation components. The alignment layer adds modest overhead, while routed retrieval keeps total latency close to lightweight baselines.
Fig. 6. Effect of removing core SEM-RAG components on held-out QA performance.
